judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are substantively equivalent, correctly identifying all three root causes and fixes with nearly identical specificity; Report 2 adds a touch more detail on transaction-scoped SET LOCAL, but this doesn't affect the graded expectations.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_owner` is the `POSTGRES_USER` from `docker-compose.yml`, which makes it the cluster **superuser**, and it also owns the tables created by `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`. Postgres exempts both superusers and table owners from RLS ... it never runs `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` ... the policy is simply never evaluated for this role — no error, no warning

report_2.evidence: In Postgres, RLS policies do not apply to a table's owner (or superuser) unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — and `0001_init.sql` only does `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, not `FORCE`. So even though `backend/app/deps.py` dutifully runs `SET app.current_tenant = ...`, the `tenant_isolation` policy is never evaluated for this connection

report_1.evidence: Point the running application at the least-privilege role: change `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` (and `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` ... keep a strict two-role split: `cedar_owner` used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` for schema changes ... and `cedar_app` for everything the app does at runtime.

report_2.evidence: Point the application's `DATABASE_URL` at the non-owner role: `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` in `.env`/`.env.example`, keeping `cedar_owner` solely for migrations ... The structural principle: two roles — a privileged owner/migration role used only by migration tooling, and a least-privilege app role (DML only, no ownership) used by all runtime code

report_1.evidence: `cedar_app` is created only by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which the README and `docs/local-setup.md` describe as a **manual, one-time, first-setup** step — it is not in `db/migrations/` and not invoked by any script. `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume ... then brings the container back up and runs only `./scripts/migrate.sh` ... So after a rebuild the schema and seed data exist but `cedar_app` does not ... To make it work again, the bootstrap must be re-run against the fresh cluster: `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`

report_2.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which per its own comment, `docs/local-setup.md`, and the README is a "one-time" manual step outside the migrations. So after a reset the freshly initialized cluster has `cedar_owner` (recreated by the Docker image from `POSTGRES_USER`) but no `cedar_app` ... To make it work again the teammate must re-run the bootstrap: `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`
